pages.text: 2. Experimental work 2.1. Materials and mix proportions For the ﬁve concrete mixtures investigated in this study, the cement, silica fume, aggregate, and water were the same. The nominal content of cement in the concretes was 700 kg/m 3. The cement used was ordinary Portland cement with a density of 3.17 g/cm 3, while the density of silica fume was 2.5 g/cm 3. The maximum particle size of aggregate was 16 mm. The amount of high-range water reducing admixture (HRWRA) was about 3.8% by weight of cement for all concrete mixtures. The mixture proportions of the plain concrete were as follows; cement: silica fume: water: natural sand (0.25–1 mm): crushed stone (0.5–8 mm): coarse aggregate (4– 16 mm) = 1: 0.15: 0.22: 0.61: 0.52: 0.93. Three different steel ﬁbres with and with- out hooked-ends were added. The properties of the steel ﬁbres are given in Table 1. Two different total volume fractions of ﬁbres, 0.75 and 1.5%, were selected. Par- tial replacement of aggregate by steel ﬁbre was based on one-to-one volume basis. The concrete mixtures were designated using the following codes: REF = plain self- compacting concrete, C0.75N = concrete with 0.5% HSS + 0.25% NSH; C0.75H = con- crete with 0.5% HSS + 0.25% HSH; C1.5N = concrete with 1.0% HSS + 0.5% NSH; C1.5H = concrete with 1.0% HSS + 0.5% HSH ( Table 2 ). 2.2. Specimen preparation In mixing, cement and silica fume were blended ﬁrst in dry condition. Then, the aggregate were added and they were mixed together. Halves of the HRWRA and the water were mixed in a pan and added into the mixture. The remaining halves of the HRWRA and the water were added into the mixture gradually to provide homoge- neity in the mixture. Steel ﬁbres were scattered in the mixture and carefully mixed to achieve a uniform distribution. After completing the fresh state tests, the speci- mens were cast in steel moulds. Details of the tests and dimensions of the speci- mens are given in Table 3 . All specimens were demoulded after 24 h, and then steam curing regimes were applied for 48 h. At the end of the ﬁrst 6 h, the temperature of steam curing reached 65 /C176C at atmospheric pressure and this condition was stabilized for the next 36 h. During the ﬁnal 6 h, the temperature of steam curing was decreased gradually to the room temperature and then the samples were kept in room condition for 24 h and further in a water tank saturated with lime at 20 /C176C until the testing day. 2.3. Test procedure 2.3.1. Flow and workability tests in fresh state In order to characterise the ﬂow and workability properties of SCC, the tradi- tional slump ﬂow, U-box, and V-funnel tests were used to measure the ﬂowability, ﬁlling and passing ability of the concrete. These testing methods have been detailed in RILEM TC 145-WSM [6] together with other possible methods. In slump ﬂow and J-ring tests, ﬂow radius (cm), the total ﬂow time (s), and ﬂow time at 500 mm spreading, t 50 (s) of the fresh concrete were measured. The ﬂow time (s) was also measured using V-funnel and U-box tests. 2.3.2. Fracture test The tests for determining the fracture energy ( GF) were performed in accor- dance with the recommendation of RILEM 50-FMC Technical Committee [9]. The deﬂection was measured using a linear variable displacement transducer (LVDT). The load was applied using a closed-loop testing machine (Instron 5500R) with a maximum capacity of 100 kN. The beams prepared for the fracture energy tests were 500 mm in length and 100 mm /C2 100 mm in cross sec

The results of the V-funnel and U-box tests are given in Fig. 4c. For both tests while the ﬂow time did not change at low ﬁbre con- tent, the addition of 1.5% ﬁbres signiﬁcantly increased the ﬂow time. Accordingly, it can be said that addition of 0.75% volume of ﬁbres did not change the ﬁlling ability of concrete signiﬁcant. The relatively high values of t50 and V-funnel tests results can be explained by the signiﬁcantly high amount of HRWRA. It is known that HRWRA entrains considerable amount of air to concrete and the air bubbles may cause higher values of t50 and V-funnel tests results. 4.2. Hardened concrete properties The hardened concrete test results (28-days old) are presented in Table 4 . The results show that while the compressive strength and modulus of elasticity of concretes did not change considerably, the addition of ﬁbres had noteworthy effects on splitting tensile and ﬂexural strengths. As expected, the increase in ﬁbre content led to an increase in these strengths. As the strength of long ﬁbres increased the ﬂexural strengths slightly increased, whereas the splitting tensile strength did not change. The most signiﬁcant ef- fect of using hybrid ﬁbres has been observed to occur on both the fracture energy and ductility ratios of concretes. It is also pre- sented in Table 4 that even when the total volume content of ﬁbres in C1.5N series is twice that of C0.75H series the fracture energies of both concretes were found to be nearly identical. This may indi- cate that the intended energies can be achieved with using high strength, but smaller amounts of steel ﬁbres. Fig. 5 shows the effects of strength and the volume of the steel ﬁbres on the load–displacement behaviour of the concretes. It is seen that the use of hybrid steel ﬁbres improves the mechanical performance and increases the fracture energy of the concretes. Especially, after the formation of the ﬁrst crack, the progress of strain hardening in the ascending branch of the curve is a typical indication of high performance concretes. The increase in the ﬁbre content signiﬁcantly increased the fracture energy of concretes. The short ﬁbres had a limited effect on the post-peak response of load versus displacement at mid-span of the beam, while there was a substantial effect of long ﬁbres on the post-peak response part of the curve, which resulted in a high value of fracture energy. The concretes with normal strength hooked-end steel ﬁbres had lower peak loads and steeper gradients of the softening branch compared to the concretes with high strength hooked-end steel ﬁ- bres. The highest fracture energies were obtained from the latter. In ﬁbre reinforced concrete, after cracking of matrices or brittle fracturing, ductile ﬁbres continue to carry and transfer the loads to other ﬁbres; hence they help keeping structural integrity and cohesion of material. The randomly distributed ﬁbres restrain crack propagation with bridging the cracks. When using high strength ﬁbres this mechanism functions more efﬁciently. In the series containing smaller amount of ﬁbres, the ﬁnal dis- placement did not reach the cut-off value of 10 mm for some of the specimens. This is probably due to the fact that there is no suf- ﬁcient amount of long ﬁbres, and some of them were slipping out from the concrete phase as seen in Fig. 6. Thus, it is also important to ﬁnd the optimum volume of ﬁbres considering that all the ﬁbres should efﬁciently contribute to the energy absorption of ﬁbre rein- forced

Mechanical behaviour and ﬁbre dispersion of hybrid steel ﬁbre reinforced self-compacting concrete Burcu Akcay a,⇑, Mehmet Ali Tasdemir b a Kocaeli University, Civil Engineering Department, 41380 Kocaeli, Turkey b Istanbul Technical University, Civil Engineering Department, 34469 Istanbul, Turkey article info Article history: Received 17 May 2011 Received in revised form 11 August 2011 Accepted 16 August 2011 Available online 20 October 2011 Keywords: Concrete ﬂowability Concrete workability Fibre strength Fibre dispersion Fracture energy Hybrid steel ﬁbres Self-compacting concrete (SCC) abstract In this study, mixture design, workability, ﬁbre dispersion/orientation, mechanical properties and frac- ture behaviour of hybrid steel ﬁbre reinforced self-compacting concretes (HSFRSCCs) were investigated. Three different types of steel ﬁbres with and/or without hooked-ends were added to the mixtures in two different volume fractions (0.75 and 1.5% of the total volume of concrete). The results of slump ﬂow, U- box, V-funnel and J-ring tests have shown that increasing the ﬁbre content of the concretes slightly reduced the workability of HSFRSCC, and the main inﬂuencing factor on ﬂowability is the geometry of ﬁbres. The addition of ﬁbres, although did not change the ﬁnal ﬂowability, decreased the rate of ﬂowabil- ity. The results from the experimental tests showed that the ﬂexural strengths increased slightly with increasing strength of long ﬁbres, whereas the splitting tensile strength remained unchanged. The con- cretes with high strength, long steel ﬁbres show behaviour of enhanced toughness and ductility com- pared to that with normal strength steel ﬁbres. The orientation and distribution of ﬁbres in concrete have been investigated by image analysis and it was observed that ﬁbres dispersed homogeneously in all concrete series without any clumping. With increasing the amount of ﬁbres, the ﬁbres were more ver- tically orientated relative to the bending loading direction, resulting in enhancement in the mechanical properties of concrete. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Because of its technical beneﬁts with the developments in new generation superplasticizers, self-compacting concrete (SCC) has been widely used in construction industry. The SCC was developed ﬁrst by Okamura and Ouchi [1]. SCC can be considered to have a liquid type of behaviour in fresh state. Main beneﬁts of using it are (i) reducing the noise caused by vibration; (ii) reducing the cost of placement; (iii) increasing the speed of construction; and (iv) decreasing the number of skilled workers. SCC can be used to overcome the compacting problems of fresh concrete, since it can be placed at every corner of formwork without vibration. High performance concrete is a deﬁnition used for concrete with high strength combined with highly enhanced durability. It is, however, known that high strength leads to an increase in the brittleness of concrete. Thus, introduction of ﬁbres in concrete, as already pointed out by a number of authors, can drastically increase the toughness of high strength concrete (e.g. [2–4] ). Micro-ﬁbre func- tions as a bridge to eliminate the formation of micro-cracks, hence the tensile strength of concrete increases. Since the micro-ﬁbres are short in size, they may be pulled out after macro-cracks are formed. The large ﬁbres have no signiﬁcant effect on preventing micro-cracking; however, when the micro-cracks condense into macro-cr

from each of the series were selected for the image analysis and the care was taken to choose samples with fracture planes almost par- allel to the loading direction. Fracture surfaces were gently smoothed and the long ﬁbres on fractures were cut without affect- ing the number of ﬁbres. The surfaces were then cleaned using ace- tone before image capturing to enhance the visibility of steel ﬁbres. Images were collected by high-resolution camera and processed by image analysis programs. As is shown in Fig. 2, the shinning objects were ﬁrst selected and the images were made binary. Since some aggregates in concrete were also selected by the program as shin- ing objects, these were extracted manually from the image. There- by, the image shown in Fig. 2c was obtained and, after ﬁtting each object to ellipse, both the size and coordinates of each ﬁbre particle (in elliptical cross-sections) were determined using the image analysis programs (ImageJ, MicroImage). Considering that the par- ticles in images are projectiles of the circular cross-section of ﬁbres, the orientation angles can be obtained ( Fig. 3 ). If the orien- tation angle is 90 /C176, ﬁbres are perpendicularly orientated to the loading direction, which means that ﬁbres can most effectively perform in fracture plane. In contrast, when the orientation angle is 0/C176, ﬁbres are parallel to the loading direction and cannot perform efﬁciently. 4. Results and evaluation 4.1. Fresh concrete properties The results of fresh state are shown and compared in Fig. 4 .I t can be deduced that addition of ﬁbres in SCC had signiﬁcant effects on t50 and total ﬂow times in both slump ﬂow and J-ring tests (Fig. 4a). The ﬂow time has increased with increasing ﬁbre volume, while the strength of longer ﬁbres has no effect on these values. It is indicated that the main inﬂuencing factor on ﬂowability is the geometry of ﬁbres rather than their strength. While the addition of 0.75% ﬁbres did not signiﬁcantly alter the t50 values of reference SCC, the addition of 1.5% of ﬁbres relatively increased the t50 val- ues, meaning that the ﬂow rate of SCC decreased with increasing ﬁbre content. Furthermore, it is also seen in Fig. 4a that for the total ﬂow time and t50 values, the J-ring tests gave higher values than the slump ﬂow tests. It is also seen that the difference between the results of J-ring tests and slump ﬂow tests becomes more sig- niﬁcant with the increasing ﬁbre content, which may indicate that the passing ability through the obstacles can be well deﬁned using the J-ring test. In addition, it is shown in Fig. 4 b that the total ﬂow radius slightly decreased with addition of ﬁbres and also the ﬁbre content did not signiﬁcantly affect the ﬂow radius. Although the ﬂow ra- dius was slightly reduced with the addition of ﬁbres, these values were still in the accepted ranges given in EFNARC Speciﬁcation and Guidelines for SCC (65–88 cm) [13]. In both tests it is concluded that the ﬂowability rate decreased with addition of ﬁbres, although the ﬁnal ﬂowability did not change for the ﬁbre contents used in the scope of this study. experimentally. For the tensile strength of ﬁbre concrete (not hy- brid mixtures), Yuguang et al. found k1 and k2 values as 0.9 and 0.04, respectively [15]. In Fig. 7, the variation in ﬂexural strength with ﬁbre content for all the tested concretes is given and the values of k1 and k2 are determined using the regression line. The regression coefﬁcients obtained are in acceptable ranges. Two different regression lines in the ﬁgure correspond to concretes with normal and high strength ﬁbres with the same k1 values (0.9). The calculations yield k2 values of 0.11 and 0.08 for concretes with high and normal strength ﬁbres, respectively. Thus, it is shown that the ﬂexural strength of hybrid ﬁbre concrete can be determined using the above calculations, although a wider range of experimental study can be conducted for better comparison of predicted values of k1 and k2. 4.3. Dispersion and orientation of ﬁbres It is known that homogeneous dispersion and orientation of ﬁ- bres have inﬂuences on fresh and hardened concrete properties especially when the amount of ﬁbres is high as is the case in this study. In order to determine the dispersion of ﬁbres we attempted to evaluate the ﬁbre density (based on number of ﬁbres) in con- crete in a certain scale using image analysis. The image area was divided into 16 /C2 16 squares and the number of ﬁbres in each square was counted. Each square was shown in different colour which represents the amount of ﬁbres. The results shown in Fig. 8 are ﬁbre dispersions representative for each of the series. As it can be seen the ﬁbres in all concretes were dispersed homo- geneously and, because of the mixture design used, there is no any local disintegration or ﬁbre clumping. Furthermore, it is seen that ﬁbre distribution in the C1.5N and C1.5H series remains uniform despite both of these series contain 1.5 volume % of ﬁbres. For the case of well and randomly distributed ﬁbres in mixtures, Stahli and Van Mier [16] stated that the more ﬁbres counted per unit square area, the more perpendicular to the cut surface is the Fig. 10. 0.25% to 0.5% increased the ductility ratio by up to 54%, while increasing the strength of ﬁbres enhanced this ratio by 46%. It can be deduced that, despite a limited enhancement in ﬂexural strength of concretes (almost two times), the increase in the dissi- pated energy may reach up to extreme values with addition of steel ﬁbres. There are some studies carried out to determine the tensile strength of hybrid ﬁbre concrete using both tensile strength of the matrix and ﬁbre content [14,15]. As is given in Table 4, addition of 0.75% of ﬁbres does not signiﬁcantly increase the ﬂexural strength of concrete, although the post-peak behaviour is changed substantially (Fig. 5). On the other hand, addition of ﬁbres at a rate of 1.5% results in substantial increase in the ﬂexural strength of concrete. Thus, following the approach by Fischer [14], we at- tempted to predict the ﬂexural strength of HSFRSCCs using the following equation: fflex ¼ k1fflexm þ k2Vf L d ð3Þ where fflexm is the ﬂexural strength of matrix, while Vf, L and d rep- resent the content, length and diameter of ﬁbre, respectively. average of these counted ﬁbres, whereas when a lesser number of ﬁbres can be counted per unit square area the average of these ﬁ- bres is oriented more parallel to the cut surface. The load–displace- ment diagram of two samples from the C0.75H series is given in Fig. 9 . Despite they are from the same series, sample-A and sam- ple-B contain 368 and 311 ﬁbres in their fractured cross-sections, respectively, and their bending loads are 5.9 and 5.1 kN, respec- tively. This indicates that the number of ﬁbres in cross-section is one of the key factors affecting the mechanical properties of ﬁbre concrete. In addition to the above investigation based on the number of ﬁbres, the alignment of ﬁbres in the fracture plane of beams was also determined. For this purpose, the geometrical properties of each ﬁbre whose coordinates in binary images already known were veriﬁed. Following the approach by Markovic [17], the orientation angle between 0 /C176and 90/C176relative to the bending loading direction was determined by taking into account the projectile of the ﬁbres on the plane. The calculated orientation angles for each series are given as a rose-diagram in Fig. 10. The radius of each nested circle interval represents the number of ﬁbres in corresponding orienta- tion angles. In concretes that contain 0.75 volume % of ﬁbres, the orientation of approximately 300 ﬁbres were determined, while this calculation was done for more than 600 ﬁbres for concretes with 1.5 volume % of ﬁbres. For investigating the concretes with hybrid ﬁbres, short ﬁbres or the ﬁbres that take very little space on the image area are ignored, and only the average values of ori- entation angles of long ﬁbres were used for better visualisation and detailed investigation ( Fig. 11 ). As already pointed out in the image analysis procedure section, if the orientation angle is about 90 /C176, ﬁbres can work efﬁciently in the fracture plane. In this study, the orientations of ﬁbres were al- most in the desirable condition, indicating an effective mixing pro- cedure with superplasticizers and self compacting during production. As is shown in Fig. 11, with increasing the amount of ﬁ- bres, the ﬁbres become more perpendicular to the loading direction, resulting in enhancement in the mechanical properties of concrete. Flexural strength and fracture energy of ﬁbre reinforced concrete, in particular, can be enhanced with homogeneous distribution of ﬁ- bres orientated in angles functioning effectively. In addition, it is known that long ﬁbres are oriented more vertically to the loading plane, which is probably due to the casting of concrete into the moulds as layers, while it is difﬁcult to control the orientation of short ﬁbres during casting [17]. 5. Conclusions Based on the tests conducted, it can be concluded that the addi- tion of ﬁbres has a slight decreasing effect on workability of SCC. It is also shown that the main inﬂuencing factor on ﬂowability and workability is the geometry of long ﬁbres rather than their strength. The ﬂowability rate decreases with addition of ﬁbres, although no signiﬁcant change in the ﬁnal ﬂowability was detected for ﬁbre contents speciﬁcally used in this study. The fracture energy test results show that the concretes with high strength long steel ﬁbres display behaviour of enhanced toughness and ductility when compared to the concretes with nor- mal strength steel ﬁbres. Concretes with normal strength ﬁbres have lower peak loads and steeper gradients of the
